g.data-narration: Welcome to Embedded System Software. Learn how specialized programs control hardware in microcontrollers for dedicated functions.
Embedded system software are specialized programs controlling hardware in devices, designed for specific dedicated functions under constraints.
g.text: Embedded System Software: Specialized programs that control hardware
components in embedded systems, designed for specific dedicated functions
within devices ranging from appliances to industrial control systems.

g.data-narration: Three main architectures exist: Monolithic (single program), Layered (HAL to Application), and Kernel-based (Microkernel vs Monolithic).
Embedded development requires specialized tools: IDEs, cross-compilers for different architectures, debuggers, and software simulators.
g.text: Cross-compilers run on host (x86) but generate code for target architecture (ARM, 8051, PIC)

g.data-narration: C dominates embedded development due to efficient code, direct memory access, and predictable execution. C++ adds OOP, Assembly for critical sections.
g.text: MISRA C provides safety-critical coding guidelines • Python/Rust emerging for specific applications

g.data-narration: The firmware lifecycle follows phases: Requirements → Design → Implementation → Testing → Debugging → Maintenance with OTA updates.
Device drivers interface software with hardware peripherals: Timers, UARTs, ADC/DACs, and GPIO pins for embedded control.
g.text: Software components providing interface between OS/application and hardware peripherals

g.data-narration: RTOS provides multitasking with deterministic timing. FreeRTOS is popular for microcontrollers. Uses priority-based preemption and task communication.
This LED blink program initializes GPIO, then toggles the LED on and off with delay loops in an infinite while loop.
This interrupt handler responds to button presses. It checks pending bits, sets flags, clears pending bits, and enables NVIC for processing.
This FreeRTOS example creates two tasks: LED blinking (priority 1) and sensor monitoring (priority 2) with vTaskDelay for timing.
The complete layered architecture flows from hardware up: Hardware → HAL → Drivers → RTOS → Middleware → Application Layer.
g.text: Application Layer - User functions and logic
RTOS or Operating System - Task scheduling
Device Drivers - UART, Timer, GPIO interfaces

g.data-narration: Know these key distinctions: Firmware (permanent in ROM) versus Software, Cross-compilation (host to target), Interrupt-driven versus Polling.
Important exam points: C is preferred for efficiency, volatile keyword for ISR variables, stack overflow prevention, and RTOS scheduling types.
g.text: Clear pending bits in interrupt handlers
Stack overflow with large local arrays

g.data-narration: In summary: Embedded software controls hardware under constraints. C dominates, layered architecture is standard, and RTOS enables multitasking.
g.text: Embedded software bridges hardware and applications under resource constraints
C language, Layered Architecture, Device Drivers, RTOS, Applications
